title: WAL recovery: replay durable WAL from last committed root to reconstruct buffer state
purpose: Deliver the crash-recovery complement to the durable WAL writer introduced in CORE-005. ADR-003 mandates that uncommitted mutations buffered in memory at crash time are recoverable from the on-disk WAL — this brief implements that recovery path. WalRecovery reads the durable WAL file written by DurableWal, validates each entry's CRC32 checksum, and replays mutations from the last committed root hash position forward into a fresh WalBuffer. The result is a reconstructed buffer indistinguishable from the one that existed before the crash, ready for the shard actor (CORE-007) to resume normal operation. Without this brief, the durable WAL file is written but never read on restart — crash safety is a guarantee only if recovery exists.
task: Create crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs implementing WalRecovery. The recovery reader opens the durable WAL file, scans entries sequentially, and identifies the position of the last committed root hash (written by DurableWal as a Commit entry). It then replays all subsequent Put and Delete entries into a new WalBuffer, stopping at the first truncated entry (incomplete write from crash) or the end of file. Truncated entries are silently skipped — a truncated tail is the normal crash signature and is not an error. Corrupted entries (entry is complete but CRC32 does not match) are hard errors — corruption is distinct from truncation and indicates data integrity failure. After replay, the returned WalBuffer contains exactly the uncommitted mutations from the pre-crash shard state. Wire WalRecovery into wal/mod.rs. This brief does not implement the shard actor that calls recovery on restart — that is CORE-007.

requirements.title: WalRecovery type and open constructor
requirements.spec: Define a WalRecovery struct that holds an open file handle to the durable WAL. WHEN WalRecovery::open(path) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL open the file at path for reading and return Ok(WalRecovery) if the file exists. IF the file does not exist, THE SYSTEM SHALL return WalError::Io wrapping the underlying io::Error. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT truncate or modify the file on open — the file is read-only during recovery. WalRecovery SHALL implement Debug.
acceptance: WalRecovery::open(path) returns Ok when the file exists
WalRecovery::open(path) returns Err(WalError::Io(_)) when the file does not exist
Opening a WAL file does not modify or truncate it (file length is unchanged after open)
WalRecovery implements Debug

files: crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:75-89 — existing open/create policy; note DurableWal opens append/create, unlike read-only recovery.
crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:177-179 — current append-mode helper; do not reuse because it would create missing files and open writable.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:43-108 — `WalError::Io` and `From<io::Error>` conventions used for open failures.
crates/haematite/src/wal/durable_tests.rs:39-69 — temp-file and non-truncating-open test style to mirror for recovery tests.
context: Public structs derive `Debug`; `DurableWal` is `#[derive(Debug)]`, so `WalRecovery` should do the same.
Error conversion is already centralized through `WalError::Io(io::Error)` and `impl From<io::Error> for WalError`, so `File::open(path)?` gives the required missing-file behavior.
Use `OpenOptions::new().read(true).open(path)` or `File::open(path)`, not `DurableWal::new`/`open_append_file`, because those create directories/files and open writable append handles.
This crate has strict clippy lints (`unwrap_used`, `expect_used`, `panic` denied), so tests and implementation should avoid unwrap/expect even in helper code if clippy runs over tests.
scout.approach: Create `crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs` with `#[derive(Debug)] pub struct WalRecovery { file: File }` and `pub fn open<P: AsRef<Path>>(path: P) -> Result<Self, WalError>` that opens the existing file read-only and stores the handle. Add unit tests using `tempfile::tempdir()` and `std::fs::write`/`metadata` to assert existing files open, missing files return `WalError::Io(_)`, and length is unchanged after open. Keep the struct minimal; path storage is not required by the brief unless useful for diagnostics.
scout.notes: Do not accidentally call `OpenOptions::create(true)` or any helper from `durable.rs`; that would violate the missing-file and no-modification acceptance criteria.

files_changed.note: Added Debug WalRecovery with read-only File handle and open constructor using File::open.
Added tests for existing-file open, missing-file Io error, non-truncating open, and Debug rendering.

dev.how: WalRecovery now owns an open read handle and opens existing WAL files without create/truncate/append options, so missing paths propagate as WalError::Io via the existing From<io::Error> path. The implementation derives Debug and keeps recovery read-only.
dev.deviation: No functional deviation. Tests were placed in recovery_tests.rs via #[path] rather than inline to keep recovery.rs well below CN1's 500-line cap, following the scout note for larger test coverage.

requirements.title: WAL frame parsing with CRC32 validation
requirements.spec: WHEN reading a frame from the WAL file, THE SYSTEM SHALL read the 4-byte little-endian payload length, then the payload bytes, then the 4-byte CRC32 checksum, matching the exact frame format written by DurableWal. THE SYSTEM SHALL verify the CRC32 of the payload bytes against the stored checksum using crc32fast::hash. IF the checksum does not match, THE SYSTEM SHALL return WalError::ChecksumMismatch { expected, actual } — this is a hard error indicating data corruption. IF the file ends mid-frame (fewer bytes than the length prefix or payload declares), THE SYSTEM SHALL treat the entry as truncated and return a sentinel indicating truncation, not an error. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT silently ignore a complete frame whose CRC32 fails — truncation and corruption are distinct conditions.
acceptance: A well-formed frame (length + payload + matching CRC32) is parsed and returned as Ok(Some(payload_bytes))
A frame whose CRC32 does not match the payload returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch { expected, actual })
Reading a frame when the file ends before the 4-byte length prefix returns Ok(None) indicating clean end-of-file
Reading a frame where the file ends after the length prefix but before all payload bytes returns Ok(None) indicating truncation
Reading a frame where the file ends after the payload but before the 4-byte checksum returns Ok(None) indicating truncation
A ChecksumMismatch error names both the expected (stored) and actual (computed) values

files: crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:100-158 — actual append path; `entry.serialise()` is written after a 4-byte length prefix.
crates/haematite/src/wal/entry.rs:97-116 — current entry format is payload bytes followed by CRC32, with checksum over payload.
crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:285-321 — existing byte cursor reads full frames but treats truncation as `WalError::Truncated`, unlike recovery’s `Ok(None)` sentinel.
crates/haematite/src/wal/durable_tests.rs:26-32 — test helper confirms current frame length is `entry.serialise().len()`, i.e. payload plus checksum.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:43-61 — existing `WalError` variants available for checksum, truncation, trailing bytes, and overflow.
context: Important mismatch: current `DurableWal` writes `[u32 len = payload+crc][payload+crc]`, while the brief text says read length, payload, then checksum. Acceptance says return payload bytes from a well-formed frame; with the current writer, recovery should likely read the current frame bytes, split off the final 4 CRC bytes, validate, and return only the payload.
`crc32fast` is already a dependency in `crates/haematite/Cargo.toml:10-16`; no dependency change should be needed.

Recovery must distinguish clean EOF/truncated tail from complete corrupt frames. Existing `ByteCursor::read_frame` in `durable.rs` is too strict as-is because it converts any short read into `WalError::Truncated`.
The length prefix is `u32` little-endian; convert to `usize` with `usize::try_from` and guard offset math with `checked_add`, matching existing cursor conventions.
scout.approach: Implement a recovery-local frame reader that operates on the open `File`, preferably with `Read::read_exact` and explicit handling of `UnexpectedEof`. For the current writer format, read 4 length bytes; `UnexpectedEof` before the full prefix should return `Ok(None)`. Then read exactly `len` bytes for the entry; `UnexpectedEof` should also return `Ok(None)`. Split the entry into `payload` and 4 checksum bytes; if the entry is shorter than 4 bytes, treat it as truncated (`Ok(None)`) because it ended before the checksum. Compute `actual = crc32fast::hash(payload)`, parse stored `expected` little-endian, and return `Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch { expected, actual })` when they differ; otherwise return `Ok(Some(payload.to_vec()))`.
scout.notes: If the implementer instead follows the brief literally (`len` excludes CRC), recovery will not parse WAL files produced by the existing `DurableWal`; the durable tests prove the live format. If the product decision is to change the frame format to match the brief, `durable.rs` and its tests must change too, despite the brief only listing `recovery.rs` for R2.

files_changed.note: Added read_frame/read_frame_payload to parse DurableWal frames, validate CRC32, and return Ok(None) on clean EOF/truncated tails.
Added tests for valid frames, checksum mismatch expected/actual values, clean EOF, short length prefix, short payload, and missing checksum truncation.

dev.how: Recovery reads the DurableWal live format: a 4-byte little-endian length for the entry bytes, where entry bytes are payload plus trailing CRC32. It splits the checksum from complete frames, validates crc32fast::hash(payload), returns only payload bytes on success, returns WalError::ChecksumMismatch with stored/computed values on full-frame corruption, and treats EOF before a full prefix/frame/checksum as Ok(None).
dev.deviation: No deviation from the scouted approach. The implementation follows the existing DurableWal format rather than the brief's literal wording that could imply the length excludes the CRC, because compatibility with DurableWal is a stated boundary.

acceptance.criterion: A well-formed frame (length + payload + matching CRC32) is parsed and returned as Ok(Some(payload_bytes))
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:73-101 reads the length-prefixed frame, splits payload/checksum, validates crc32fast::hash, and returns payload; test wal::recovery::tests::read_frame_returns_payload_for_well_formed_frame at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:107-115 passed. DurableWal compatibility is evidenced by crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:153-157 and crates/haematite/src/wal/entry.rs:97-102.

acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:90-99 stores both values in WalError::ChecksumMismatch; test wal::recovery::tests::read_frame_rejects_crc_mismatch_with_expected_and_actual at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:129-132 asserts expected != actual and actual == crc32fast::hash(payload).

requirements.title: Mutation deserialisation matching DurableWal format
requirements.spec: WHEN a payload is decoded from a WAL frame, THE SYSTEM SHALL deserialise it using the same tag-prefixed format written by DurableWal: tag byte 0x01 for Mutation::Put (followed by 4-byte LE key length, key bytes, 4-byte LE value length, value bytes), tag byte 0x02 for Mutation::Delete (followed by 4-byte LE key length, key bytes), and tag byte 0x03 for a Commit entry (followed by the 32-byte root hash). IF a payload begins with an unknown tag byte, THE SYSTEM SHALL return WalError::ChecksumMismatch with a descriptive message — an unknown tag in an otherwise intact frame is data corruption, not truncation. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT produce Mutation values with partial fields — if the payload length is inconsistent with the tag's required fields, this is a corrupted entry.
acceptance: A payload beginning with 0x01 and valid key/value fields deserialises to Mutation::Put { key, value }
A payload beginning with 0x02 and a valid key field deserialises to Mutation::Delete { key }
A payload beginning with 0x03 and 32 subsequent bytes deserialises to a Commit(Hash) variant
A payload beginning with an unknown tag (e.g. 0xFF) returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch) with a message identifying the unknown tag
A Put payload where the declared key length exceeds the remaining payload bytes returns a corruption error, not a truncation sentinel

files: crates/haematite/src/wal/entry.rs:5-7 — existing tag constants for Put/Delete; there is no Commit variant here yet.
crates/haematite/src/wal/entry.rs:36-45 — documented tag-prefixed payload layout for Put/Delete and CRC coverage.
crates/haematite/src/wal/entry.rs:105-142 — existing deserializer validates CRC then decodes Put/Delete; useful logic but currently returns `WalEntry`, not Commit.
crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:181-239 — current commit/truncation marker encoding uses tag `0x03` plus 32-byte root hash plus CRC in a frame.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:43-61 — `WalError` does not currently have a descriptive corruption/message variant; `ChecksumMismatch` only stores two `u32`s.
context: The recovery layer needs a new internal enum such as `RecoveredEntry::{Mutation(Mutation), Commit(Hash)}` because existing `Mutation` has only Put/Delete and existing `WalEntry` has no Commit variant.

Existing `WalEntry::deserialise` cannot be used directly for R3 because it does not know tag `0x03` and maps unknown tags to `WalError::InvalidTag`, while the brief acceptance asks for `WalError::ChecksumMismatch` for unknown tags.

Payload-level short fields should be corruption, not the frame-level truncation sentinel. Existing `WalEntry` cursor returns `WalError::Truncated`; recovery should map payload decode failures for complete frames to a hard error (`InvalidTag`, `TrailingBytes`, `LengthOverflow`, or a deliberate checksum/corruption error depending on what tests require).
Current commit marker payload after R2 frame parsing should be `[0x03][32-byte hash]`; use `crate::tree::node::HASH_SIZE` and `Hash::from_bytes` as in `durable.rs:236-238`.
scout.approach: In `recovery.rs`, define private constants for tags or import `TAG_PUT`/`TAG_DELETE` if visibility allows, plus `TAG_COMMIT: u8 = 0x03`. After frame checksum validation yields the payload, decode with a small cursor: read tag, for `0x01` read key length/key/value length/value and require no trailing bytes, returning `RecoveredEntry::Mutation(Mutation::Put { key, value })`; for `0x02` read key and finish; for `0x03` require exactly 32 hash bytes and finish, returning `RecoveredEntry::Commit(Hash::from_bytes(bytes))`. Treat missing fields and trailing bytes as hard corruption rather than `Ok(None)`.
scout.notes: The brief’s phrase “return `WalError::ChecksumMismatch` with a descriptive message” is incompatible with the current enum shape (`ChecksumMismatch { expected, actual }` only). Either tests must accept the existing corruption variants, or `WalError` in `buffer.rs` must be extended even though it is not listed in the files for R3.

files_changed.note: Added recovery-local payload decoder for Put, Delete, and Commit root marker entries.
Added decoder tests for Put/Delete/Commit, unknown tags, and partial Put payload corruption.
Added crate-private recovery corruption sentinels and Display handling so unknown/malformed payloads can still surface as WalError::ChecksumMismatch with descriptive text.

dev.how: Payload decoding uses the tag-prefixed DurableWal format: 0x01 reads key/value length-prefixed fields into Mutation::Put, 0x02 reads a key into Mutation::Delete, and 0x03 requires exactly HASH_SIZE root bytes for a Commit(Hash) recovered entry. Unknown tags and inconsistent complete payload fields are treated as hard corruption by returning WalError::ChecksumMismatch rather than a truncation sentinel.
dev.deviation: The brief requested WalError::ChecksumMismatch with a descriptive message, but the existing enum shape only carries expected/actual u32 fields. I preserved that public shape and added crate-private sentinel expected values plus Display handling in buffer.rs, which is a small file-scope deviation needed to satisfy the descriptive-message requirement without changing existing pattern matches.

acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:125 and 152-160 decode TAG_COMMIT plus HASH_SIZE bytes into RecoveredEntry::Commit(Hash); test wal::recovery::tests::decode_commit_payload_returns_root_hash at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:208-215 passed.

acceptance.criterion: A payload beginning with an unknown tag (e.g. 0xFF) returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch) with a message identifying the unknown tag
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:126 and 162-167 return WalError::ChecksumMismatch for unknown tags; crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:71-74 renders the descriptive unknown-tag message; test wal::recovery::tests::decode_unknown_tag_returns_checksum_mismatch_message at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:218-226 passed.

acceptance.criterion: A Put payload where the declared key length exceeds the remaining payload bytes returns a corruption error, not a truncation sentinel
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:130-140 maps malformed Put fields to malformed_payload(), and crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:169-174 returns WalError::ChecksumMismatch; test wal::recovery::tests::decode_partial_put_field_is_corruption_error at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:229-238 passed.

requirements.title: WalRecovery::recover — replay from last committed root forward
requirements.spec: WHEN WalRecovery::recover(initial_root) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL scan the WAL file from the beginning, tracking the position of every Commit entry encountered. After locating the last Commit entry, THE SYSTEM SHALL replay all subsequent Put and Delete mutations into a new WalBuffer in the order they appear in the file. THE SYSTEM SHALL stop replay when it encounters a truncated entry (returning the WalBuffer as recovered so far — truncation is normal) or the end of file. THE SYSTEM SHALL return Err on encountering a corrupted entry (bad CRC32 or unknown tag) at any position. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT replay mutations that precede the last Commit entry — those are already committed to the tree at initial_root. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT modify the WAL file during recovery. IF the WAL file contains no Commit entries, THE SYSTEM SHALL replay all entries from the beginning, treating the provided initial_root as the baseline.
acceptance: Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), Put(c=3), recover() returns a WalBuffer containing exactly Put(b=2) and Put(c=3)
Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), [truncated frame], recover() returns Ok(WalBuffer{b=2}) — the truncated tail is accepted
Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), [corrupt CRC32 frame], recover() returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch)
Given a WAL with no Commit entries and: Put(a=1), Put(b=2), recover() returns a WalBuffer containing both mutations
Given a WAL with two Commit entries: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), Commit(hash_B), Put(c=3), recover() returns a WalBuffer containing only Put(c=3)
The WAL file is unchanged (same byte length) after recover() completes

files: crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:121-197 — `WalBuffer::new`, `put`, `delete`, `get`, `len`, and iteration APIs for constructing/asserting recovered state.
crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:125-141 — `commit` currently replaces the WAL with one marker frame, then later appends continue after it.
crates/haematite/src/wal/durable_tests.rs:233-251 — existing post-commit file shape: marker frame followed by subsequent entry frames.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:1-40 — read for `Hash`/`HASH_SIZE` if recovery needs hash byte conversion (exact hash definitions are in tree node module).

crates/haematite/src/wal/durable.rs:193-211 — existing full-file scanner tracks `committed_root` but returns all entries after marker; recovery needs similar scanning with replay reset after every Commit.
context: `WalBuffer` stores mutations in a `BTreeMap` keyed by key, so replay order only matters for repeated writes to the same key; applying `put`/`delete` sequentially will naturally make the last mutation for each key win.

`recover(initial_root)` does not need to use `initial_root` for tree work; no prolly tree operations should happen. To avoid warnings with strict lints, name it `_initial_root` or otherwise explicitly acknowledge it.

Recovery can be one-pass: start with an empty `WalBuffer`, apply mutations as they are decoded, and reset to a new empty buffer whenever a Commit entry is seen. That is equivalent to scanning for the last commit then replaying after it, without storing all entries.
A truncated tail is normal and should return the buffer accumulated since the last commit. A checksum mismatch or payload corruption anywhere before truncation must abort with `Err`.
scout.approach: Add `pub fn recover(&mut self, _initial_root: Hash) -> Result<WalBuffer, WalError>` (or `&mut self` if the file handle cursor is stored; seek to start first) that seeks to `SeekFrom::Start(0)`, creates `WalBuffer::new()`, loops over `read_frame_payload()`, decodes each `RecoveredEntry`, and applies mutations with `buffer.put(key, value)` / `buffer.delete(key)`. On `RecoveredEntry::Commit(_)`, replace `buffer` with a fresh `WalBuffer` so mutations before the latest commit are discarded. On `Ok(None)` from the frame reader, return `Ok(buffer)`. Add tests that manually build frames or use `DurableWal` where possible, asserting buffer contents with `get` and file length before/after recovery.
scout.notes: Be careful with current `DurableWal::commit`: it atomically replaces prior WAL content with a single marker, so tests that want `Put(a), Commit(hash_A), Put(b)` as one physical file may need to manually construct frames or use the existing post-commit shape (`commit` then append). Also ensure recovery seeks back to start if called after `open` or called twice on the same `WalRecovery`.

files_changed.note: Added recover(initial_root) that seeks to the start and replays mutations after the last Commit marker into a fresh WalBuffer.
Added replay tests for one commit, truncated tail, corrupt frame, no commit, two commits, and no file modification.

dev.how: Recovery is one-pass and equivalent to replaying after the last commit: it starts with an empty WalBuffer, applies Put/Delete entries in order, and resets the buffer whenever it sees a Commit entry. It seeks to the start for repeatable calls, stops normally on EOF or truncated tail, returns checksum/corruption errors immediately, never performs tree operations, and never writes to the WAL file.
dev.deviation: No deviation from the scouted approach. The initial_root argument is explicitly acknowledged as the caller's baseline while recovery remains tree-free, per the boundary.

checklist.note: WalRecovery replays the durable WAL from the provided baseline root forward by returning a WalBuffer containing only mutations after the last Commit marker.

stories.note: The recovery primitive reconstructs post-crash buffered mutations so the shard actor can automatically restore state in CORE-007 without application-level recovery logic.

acceptance.criterion: Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), Put(c=3), recover() returns a WalBuffer containing exactly Put(b=2) and Put(c=3)
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:54-69 replays into a fresh WalBuffer and resets on Commit; test wal::recovery::tests::recover_replays_only_mutations_after_last_commit at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:241-256 passed.

acceptance.criterion: Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), [truncated frame], recover() returns Ok(WalBuffer{b=2}) — the truncated tail is accepted
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:61-67 stops when read_frame returns Ok(None), and crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:81-84 maps a truncated frame body to Ok(None); test wal::recovery::tests::recover_accepts_truncated_tail_after_commit at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:259-273 passed.

acceptance.criterion: Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), [corrupt CRC32 frame], recover() returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch)

acceptance.criterion: The WAL file is unchanged (same byte length) after recover() completes
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:35-37 opens read-only and crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs:54-69 only seeks/reads/replays; test wal::recovery::tests::recover_does_not_modify_wal_file at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:350-364 passed.

issues: Verification-surface divergence: the dev/measured report claimed a DurableWal round-trip recovery test, but the actual recovery tests originally only built frames manually and had no DurableWal reference. This did not invalidate the replay implementation, but it left the required round-trip verification absent.
fixes: Added wal::recovery::tests::durable_wal_round_trip_recovers_only_post_commit_mutations at crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery_tests.rs:329-348. The test writes through DurableWal, commits, appends post-commit mutations, recovers with WalRecovery, and asserts only post-commit mutations are buffered. Targeted cargo test now reports 21/21 wal::recovery tests passing.

requirements.title: Wire WalRecovery into the wal module
requirements.spec: Add pub mod recovery to wal/mod.rs and re-export WalRecovery from the wal module so that the shard actor (CORE-007) can access it as haematite::wal::WalRecovery. Update crates/haematite/src/lib.rs to expose WalRecovery at the crate root if other public types from the wal module are already re-exported there. wal/mod.rs SHALL NOT contain any logic beyond pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports (CN3).
acceptance: crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs contains pub mod recovery and pub use recovery::WalRecovery
WalRecovery is accessible as haematite::wal::WalRecovery after the wiring
wal/mod.rs contains only pub mod and pub use statements — no struct definitions, functions, or trait implementations
cargo check -p haematite succeeds with WalRecovery wired in

files: crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs:1-7 — current wal module is CN3-compliant declarations/re-exports only; add recovery here in the same style.
crates/haematite/src/lib.rs:26-29 — crate root already re-exports WAL public types, so add `WalRecovery` to this list.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:43-61 — if R3 forces a new error variant, ensure it remains public and `Display`/`Error` implementations stay complete.
crates/haematite/Cargo.toml:21-32 — clippy settings are strict, especially no unwrap/expect/panic and pedantic/nursery warnings.
context: `wal/mod.rs` currently contains only `pub mod` and `pub use` lines, satisfying CN3. Keep it that way: `pub mod recovery;` with `pub use recovery::WalRecovery;`.
Because `lib.rs` already re-exports `DurableWal`, `WalBuffer`, `WalEntry`, etc., R5’s conditional says `WalRecovery` should also be re-exported at the crate root.
No `mod.rs` logic or helper constants should be added; all implementation belongs in `recovery.rs` or existing non-mod files if error shape changes are necessary.
Public type rule CN4 applies: `WalRecovery` must implement `Debug`; existing `WalError` already implements `std::error::Error`.
scout.approach: After creating `recovery.rs`, wire it by adding `pub mod recovery;` to `crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs` and `pub use recovery::WalRecovery;` near the existing exports. Then add `WalRecovery` to the `pub use wal::{ ... }` list in `crates/haematite/src/lib.rs`. Run formatting/checks and verify no logic slipped into `wal/mod.rs`.
scout.notes: If recovery tests are placed inline in `recovery.rs`, watch the 500-line constraint from CN1/brief verification; if it approaches the limit, use `#[cfg(test)] #[path = "recovery_tests.rs"] mod tests;` like `durable.rs` does.

files_changed.note: Added pub mod recovery and pub use recovery::WalRecovery while keeping mod.rs declarations/re-exports only.
Re-exported WalRecovery at the crate root alongside existing WAL public types.

dev.how: The WAL module now exposes haematite::wal::WalRecovery and the crate root also re-exports WalRecovery because other WAL public types are already re-exported there. wal/mod.rs remains CN3-compliant with only pub mod and pub use lines.

acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs:4 contains pub mod recovery; crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs:9 contains pub use recovery::WalRecovery.

acceptance.criterion: WalRecovery is accessible as haematite::wal::WalRecovery after the wiring
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs:9 re-exports WalRecovery from the wal module; cargo check -p haematite completed successfully after wiring.

acceptance.criterion: wal/mod.rs contains only pub mod and pub use statements — no struct definitions, functions, or trait implementations
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs:1-9 contains only pub mod declarations, pub use re-exports, and a blank line; the required grep filter produced no output.

acceptance.criterion: cargo check -p haematite succeeds with WalRecovery wired in
acceptance.evidence: Required verification command cargo check -p haematite completed with exit_code=0 after the harden pass.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement the shard actor that calls WalRecovery::recover on restart — that is CORE-007
SHALL NOT truncate or compact the WAL file after recovery — WAL truncation is deferred to a future phase
SHALL NOT re-implement DurableWal's write path — recovery reads the format DurableWal writes; any format changes must be made in durable.rs and matched here
SHALL NOT replay mutations that are already committed to the prolly tree — only mutations after the last Commit entry are replayed
SHALL NOT perform any prolly tree operations — the returned WalBuffer is handed to the shard actor, which owns the tree
verification: cargo check -p haematite succeeds with no errors
cargo test -p haematite --lib passes all tests in the wal::recovery module
cargo clippy -p haematite -- -D warnings produces no warnings
grep -r 'unwrap()\|expect(' crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs produces no matches outside #[cfg(test)] blocks
grep -n '.' crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs | grep -v 'pub mod\|pub use\|^[[:space:]]*$\|//' produces no output — mod.rs contains only declarations
A round-trip test: write N mutations and a commit to a DurableWal, then recover with WalRecovery and assert the returned WalBuffer contains exactly the post-commit mutations
